agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being scoped for customer-facing delivery visibility. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements regarding whether courier GPS location should be exposed to customers. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that must be resolved before any implementation can proceed.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver/delivery person physically transporting the package from origin to destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"GPS coordinates of the courier's device, updated at high frequency during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery visibility","definition":"The degree to which a customer can observe the progress of their in-transit package."},{"term":"Package tracking","definition":"The ability to monitor a shipment's progress, which may or may not include courier-level GPS granularity."},{"term":"Active delivery window","definition":"The time period from dispatch/pickup to final delivery confirmation, during which tracking is relevant."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (traffic, break) — exact GPS reveals personal behavior to customer","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until core conflict is resolved, but any solution must account for privacy during non-movement periods."},{"scenario":"Courier is multi-dropping (carrying multiple customers' packages) — location reveals service to other customers","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until core conflict is resolved, but multi-drop privacy is a secondary concern that compounds the primary conflict."},{"scenario":"Courier goes off-route for personal reasons during a delivery","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS would expose this; abstracted tracking would not. Both have business implications (accountability vs. privacy)."},{"scenario":"Customer uses exact GPS to intercept or confront courier","expected_behavior":"Safety concern that supports Operations' position — must be weighed in resolution."},{"scenario":"Regulatory environment (GDPR, local labor law) restricts sharing employee location with third parties","expected_behavior":"Legal review required. In many jurisdictions, sharing exact worker GPS with customers requires explicit worker consent and may be restricted by labor regulations."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High impact either way. Customer Success argues visibility drives customer retention and competitive differentiation (revenue protection). Operations argues driver attrition from surveillance is causing recruitment/retention costs to spike (cost increase). Both are revenue-material — the conflict is real, not manufactured.","operational":"Showing exact GPS increases support volume when customers see couriers 'not moving' or going off-route. Not showing location increases 'where is my package' support contacts. Either extreme has operational cost.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability — knowing when to be available. Couriers want autonomy and dignity — not being surveilled by strangers. Both are legitimate user needs in a two-sided marketplace.","risk":"CRITICAL: Building to either team's spec without resolving the conflict guarantees a business failure mode. Ship exact GPS → driver churn accelerates. Ship no visibility → customer satisfaction drops. Legal risk is also unquantified — sharing worker GPS with customers may violate labor/privacy regulations in operating jurisdictions.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery predictability. Exact GPS is one way to provide it, but not the only way. ETA-based or zone-based tracking may satisfy the core need without requiring exact coordinates."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected. Exact GPS sharing creates a surveillance dynamic that is already causing attrition. Their safety and dignity are non-negotiable operational requirements in a labor-constrained market."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Their requirement may be over-specified — the underlying need is 'transparency' which can be achieved through multiple mechanisms."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver retention and fleet capacity. Their blanket 'never show location' may also be over-specified — the underlying need is 'protect driver privacy and retention'."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED. Sharing exact employee GPS coordinates with external parties has GDPR, labor law, and potentially local regulatory implications that neither team has addressed."},{"group":"Product/Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a business strategy decision (two-sided marketplace balance), not a feature decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the two authoritative stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory non-negotiable requirements. This is not an ambiguity or a gap — it is a hard contradiction at the business policy level. No technically correct implementation exists until leadership resolves which business constraint takes priority, or approves a compromise approach (e.g., zone-based tracking, ETA-only, consent-based GPS sharing). Additionally, legal/regulatory review has not been performed, which could invalidate one or both positions entirely.","high_confidence_areas":["Both teams have legitimate business concerns","This is a business policy conflict, not a technical design question","A compromise solution space exists (zone-based, ETA-based, consent-based) that neither team has explored","Legal review is mandatory before any implementation"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which business priority takes precedence (customer transparency vs. driver retention)","Whether exact GPS is legally permissible in all operating jurisdictions","Whether the Customer Success team's actual need requires exact GPS or just delivery predictability","The quantified driver attrition impact vs. quantified customer satisfaction impact"],"assumptions":["Both teams are accurately representing their stakeholders' concerns","No executive decision has already been made that one team is unaware of","The courier platform operates as a two-sided marketplace where both customer and driver satisfaction are business-critical"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact courier GPS be shown to customers at all times. Operations requires courier location NEVER be shown to customers. These are directly contradictory, non-negotiable requirements from two authoritative teams. No implementation can satisfy both simultaneously. This MUST be resolved at the executive/product leadership level before any development begins.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers/Drivers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Product leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS (risks accelerating driver attrition, potential legal exposure)","B: Side with Operations — show no location at all (risks customer satisfaction decline, competitive disadvantage)","C: Compromise — implement zone-based or ETA-based tracking that gives customers delivery predictability without exposing exact courier coordinates","D: Consent-based model — allow couriers to opt in/out of precise location sharing, with incentive structure","E: Graduated visibility — show package location (coarse zone) not courier location, preserving privacy while providing transparency"],"recommendation":"Option C or E. The underlying customer need is delivery predictability, not surveillance capability. Zone-based tracking ('Your courier is 2 minutes away' or 'Your courier is in your neighborhood') satisfies the transparency goal without creating the surveillance dynamic that drives attrition. This reframes the problem from a zero-sum conflict to a design challenge. However, this is a BUSINESS STRATEGY decision that must be made by product leadership — not by engineering or either requesting team. Legal review is also mandatory before any location-sharing feature ships."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal and regulatory review of sharing courier GPS data with customers has not been performed. In many jurisdictions (EU/GDPR, California/CCPA, various labor laws), sharing exact employee location data with third parties requires explicit consent and may be restricted. This could make Option A (exact GPS) legally impermissible regardless of business preference.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers/Drivers","Product leadership"],"options":["Engage legal counsel to review location-sharing permissibility in all operating jurisdictions before any design work","Proceed with privacy-preserving design (zone/ETA-based) that minimizes legal risk by default"],"recommendation":"Engage legal review immediately. Do not design for exact GPS sharing until legal confirms it is permissible. Default to privacy-preserving approaches."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot proceed with implementation of either team's requirement as stated","business_rule_broken":"The two requirements are mutually exclusive. Implementing either one as specified would violate the other team's stated non-negotiable constraint. Executive arbitration or a reframed solution is required before acceptance criteria can be defined."}]}
